segments.s747-g41: ورا is the classical object/indirect pronoun 'to him' (vora = 'to/for him'); نام is the noun 'name'; شیروی here functions as an epithet/name ('Shiruy', literally lion-like/brave) attached to نام; بد is the negative adjective 'bad/ill' forming the epithet 'Bad-Shiruy'; آشکار is an adjective 'clear, manifest'. The whole phrase means 'to him the name "Bad-Shiruy" was made manifest.'
segments.s747-g42: قبادش is the proper name Qobad with the third-person object/possessive suffix -ش (here functioning like 'Qobad (of him/for him)' but contextually 'Qobad' as subject); همی‌خواند is the old epic imperfect/habitual tense formed with همی- + خواند, meaning 'used to call/kept calling' (a repeated or customary past action); این پیشکار = 'this attendant' (این = 'this, the foregoing'; پیشکار = 'attendant'). Together: 'Qobad, this attendant, would call him that.'
segments.s747-g43: شب تیره = 'dark/ominous night' (adjective after noun, typical Persian word order is noun + adjective); باید شدن is an impersonal/modal verbal phrase 'must happen / must be' (باید + infinitive/شدن) indicating necessity; سوی چین = 'toward China' (سوی = 'toward', چین = 'China'). The clause reads 'a dark night must befall/must happen toward China.'
segments.s747-g44: وگر is the conditional 'but if / or if'; سوی ما چین = 'toward our China' or 'toward us, (to) China' (ما is 'we/us' and here marks the perspective); و مکران زمین links another place 'and the land of Makran' (مکران = Makran, زمین = 'land'). The clause offers an alternative direction: 'or if toward us, toward China and the land of Makran.'
segments.s747-g45: بریشان is بر + ایشان (here fused) meaning 'against them / upon them' (classical bound-preposition + pronoun); به افسون is an adverbial phrase 'by/with enchantment' (به + noun used adverbially); بگیریم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive form of گرفتن used here with a hortatory/future sense 'we will seize / we shall take'; راه is the direct object 'the road/way'. The clause means 'we will, by enchantment, seize/cut off their road.'
segments.s747-g46: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; فغفور is the title 'faghfur' (Byzantine/Caesar-like emperor); چینی here qualifies that ruler as 'Chinese' (i.e. 'the Chinese faghfur' or 'the faghfur of the Chinese'); بخواهم is 1st-person-singular present/subjunctive of خواستن used with future/intentional force 'I will ask/request'; سپاه is the object 'an army/troops'. So: 'I will request an army from the Chinese faghfur.'
segments.s747-g47: ازان is a literary/poetic form 'from that / because of that'; کاخترش is کاختر (pigeon/dove) with the third-person possessive suffix -ش 'his pigeon'; به آسمان 'toward/on the sky'; تیره بود is the past copula 'was dark/ominous' (تیره + بود). The clause reads literally 'from that his pigeon was dark to the sky' — i.e. 'his pigeon was dark against the sky' (an image/omen).
segments.s747-g48: سخنهای او = 'his words/speeches' (سخن with plural -های and possessive او); بر زمین = 'upon the ground/earth' (بر = 'on/upon'); خیره بود uses خیره in the sense 'astonished/stunned' (here predicative) + بود 'was' (past). Together: 'his words lay astonished/upon the earth' — a poetic way to say his words fell silent or were struck dumb.
segments.s747-g81: را is the definite/object marker attached to یکی (object = 'one, someone'). همی is an archaic/poetic continuous or habitual marker placed before the verb (here giving an ongoing or customary sense). تاج شاهی is a genitive/compound: 'tāj-e shāhi' = 'the crown of kingship' (tāj modified by شاهی). دهد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive/literary form 'he gives'. The whole order is: object (یکی را) + adverbial/modal particle (همی) + direct object/phrase (تاج شاهی) + verb (دهد).
segments.s747-g82: یکی functions again as an object 'another person'; را به (here written رابه) combines the object marker را with the preposition به 'to/into', marking transfer toward the following goal. دریا is the goal 'sea'; به ماهی literally 'as/to a fish'—به here indicates manner/role (like 'as' or 'into the condition of'); دهد is the same 3rd-person present. Word order: (object) + (را + preposition) + goal + manner + verb.
segments.s747-g83: یکی را is the object phrase again. برهنه is an adjective 'naked/bare' that applies to the coordinated nouns سر و پای ('head and feet'); سفت is another adjective (here 'tight, firm' or 'bound tightly') coordinating with the preceding descriptors. The sequence is object → descriptive adjectives/nouns describing the object's condition.
segments.s747-g84: This is a negated list introduced by نه ... و ... و نه (neither ... nor ...). آرام functions as a noun 'rest/peace' here; خورد is used as 'food/eating' (here indicating 'sustenance' or 'a meal'); جای is 'place'; نهفت ('hidden/concealed') functions adjectivally to yield 'a place to hide' or 'a hiding-place'. Grammatically the line enumerates things the person lacks: no rest, no food, and no hiding-place.
segments.s747-g85: یکی را is the object. دهد is the 3rd-person singular literary present 'he gives'. نوشه, شهد and شیر are coordinated direct objects (a draught/nectar, honey, and milk) listed after the verb. The verb governs the list of gifts given to that one.
segments.s747-g86: بپوشد is the literary 3rd-person singular (be- prefix + پوشد) meaning 'he clothes/robes (him)'. به introduces the material or garment in which the person is clothed ('in' or 'with'). دیبا و خز و حریر are coordinated nouns (brocade, velvet/fur, silk). Word order: verb (بپوشد) + prepositional phrase (به ...) specifying the clothes/materials.
segments.s747-g87: سرانجام is an adverb 'finally/in the end'. هردو is the subject 'both'. به خاک is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the dust/earth' (idiomatically 'in the grave'/dead). اندرند is an archaic literary present plural meaning 'they are (inside)' or 'they lie (in)'. The clause states the eventual fate: both end up in the earth.
segments.s747-g88: به introduces the locative/scene 'in' or 'into'. تاریک modifies دام 'dark trap', and هلاک further qualifies دام as 'trap of destruction/doom' (a possessive or descriptive cluster). اندرند repeats the archaic 'they are'. Syntactically this is a second statement of the same fate in metaphorical terms: 'they are in the dark snare of doom.'
segments.s747-g25: بدو is a prepositional/dative phrase 'to him' placed before the speech verb; گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (the supplied senses did not list the verbal sense). شاها is a vocative 'O king' addressing the king; چه شاید بدن is an interrogative fragment 'what can be (done) about the body' with چه 'what' and شاید 'perhaps/it is possible'.
segments.s747-g26: برین is the fused phrase بر + این ('upon this') with ین as an orthographic variant of این; داستانی is the noun 'tale/affair' (here 'this matter'); بباید is the modal 'must/ought' and is followed by the infinitive زدن, so بباید زدن = 'one must do/strike (act upon)'.
segments.s747-g27: از + آواز او is an ablative phrase 'from/at the sound of his voice'; شاه is the subject 'the king' and بیدار شد is the compound past/resultative 'became awake / woke up' (شد as auxiliary/verb 'became').
segments.s747-g28: دلش is 'his heart' with the possessive suffix ش; زان سخن = ز + آن + سخن 'from/for that speech'; پر ز آزار شد is a poetic construction 'became full of distress' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = poetic 'of/from', شد = 'became').
segments.s747-g29: به شیرین is 'to/for Shirin' (به marking the indirect object); چنین گفت is a formula 'thus he said'; کای is the vocative particle 'O'; ماه روی is a poetic epithet 'moon-faced one' (ماه = beautiful/‘moon’, روی = face).
segments.s747-g30: چه داری بخواب اندرون is a colloquial/poetic question: چه + داری (here the auxiliary 'are (doing)') + بخواب (verbal root 'to sleep' indicating the action) + اندرون 'inside/within' — together 'why are you sleeping inside?'. گفت وگوی at the end is a terse verbal noun/phrase related to 'speech/conversation' but the immediate clause finishes with the question; گفت is again the reporting verb (verbal sense not listed).
segments.s747-g31: بدو گفت شیرین places the dative 'to her' (بدو) before the reporting verb (گفت) and the addressee (شیرین). که introduces the subordinate clause; بگشای is an imperative 'open' (2nd person) and گوش here means 'ear' or 'attention' (imperative formula 'open your ear' = 'listen').
segments.s747-g32: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'the crying/outcry' or 'to cry out' (here rendered as a noun: 'the cry'); پاسبانان is the plural 'the guards' as the agent of that cry; نیوش in this line is not used with any of the literal senses given in the list (the supplied senses are unrelated), so its exact lexical sense here is irregular/archaic or not matched by the provided senses.
segments.s747-g49: شب تیره افسون forms a compact poetic noun phrase: شب تیره = 'dark night' and افسون 'enchantment' qualifies it; the verb phrase نیامد به کار ('did not come to use') is an idiom meaning 'was of no use'. نیامد is simple past negative and the light-weight prepositional phrase به کار ('to/for use') follows the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s747-g50: همی‌آمدش is an archaic/progressive form: همی‌آمد + ش (enclitic) = 'kept coming to him' (continuous past toward him). کار دشوار خوار is a noun with two following adjectives ('a task difficult and humbling'); Persian often places multiple modifiers after the noun here.
segments.s747-g51: به شیرین is a dative phrase 'to Shirin'; چنین گفت is 'said thus' where چنین ('thus') precedes the verb گفت (here used as 'said'). که introduces the content clause that follows: آمد زمان = 'the time has come' (آمد is past simple of 'to come').
segments.s747-g52: بر افسون ما چیره شد is built around the verb phrase چیره شدن بر (to prevail/overcome over X): چیره شد = 'became dominant/overcame', with افسون ما ('our enchantment') as the object of بر. بدگمان at the end names the troubling force that has prevailed (i.e. 'suspicion'/'doubt'), functioning as the subject/agent now dominant.
segments.s747-g53: بدو = 'to him' (dative) comes before گفت; word order here is emphatic/poetic: 'to him said Shirin'. که introduces the reported phrase; نوشه بدی = 'a draught/portion of badness' (poetic metaphor meaning a taste/portion of harm).
segments.s747-g54: همیشه ز تو دور دست بدی literally orders constituents: همیشه 'always', ز تو 'from you' (poetic ز for از), دور دست بدی = 'the hand/power of evil is far' (دور modifies دست). دست here means 'power/agency', so the whole clause says 'may the power/hand of evil always be far from you.'
segments.s747-g55: بدانش is an imperative-like 'know this' (بدان + enclitic ش = 'this'), followed by کنون 'now'. چاره خوی ش ساز = 'prepare/make your remedy': چاره 'remedy/means', خوی + ش (possessive) = 'your own', and ساز is an imperative 'make/prepare' (classical/poetic verb form).
segments.s747-g56: مبادا introduces a negative exhortation 'lest/may it not be that...' and the subordinate clause که آید به دشمن نیاز uses آید (present/subjunctive sense 'come') with the phrase به دشمن نیاز ('to/for the enemy, need/supplication'); together the clause warns 'lest need should come to the enemy' i.e. avoid a situation in which the enemy comes needing/has cause to come (contextually: to approach the palace).
segments.s747-g1: همان (’that/same’) points back to a previously mentioned person; زاد here is used as a substantive/adjectival noun ‘born/of birth’ (i.e., ‘the one born/offspring’), so زاد فرخ = ‘the fortunate-born’. بدرگاه بر is an inverted classical word order meaning ‘at the court’ (بدرگاه = court/presence; the particle بر precedes بود in the next clause).
segments.s747-g2: همی‌بود is the older imperfective/past progressive form (همی‑ + بود) meaning ‘was/continued to be’ (habitual or ongoing past). و = ‘and’. کس را is the indefinite object with را marking the object (‘any person’ as object). ندادی is the simple past negative of دادن (‘did not give’), so کس را ندادی گذر = ‘gave no one passage’; گذر is the noun ‘passage’/‘permission to pass’.
segments.s747-g3: که introduces the subordinate temporal/reason clause here (‘when/that’). آگه شدی literally ‘became aware’ (آگه = aware, شدی = became/was made aware); in classical narrative this can be rendered impersonally (‘when he learned’ or ‘when news reached him’). زان سخن شهریار = ‘of that speech/word of the king’ (زان = ‘from/of that’ + سخن = ‘word/saying’ + شهریار = ‘the king’).
segments.s747-g4: به درگاه = ‘to/at the court’ (به + درگاه). بر بود is an archaic/poetic ordering of the verb phrase meaning ‘was (standing) at (the court)’ — بر (cf. earlier) pairs with بود. چون = ‘like/as’ introducing a simile, and پرده‌دار = ‘curtain-keeper; chamberlain’ (a court officer). The whole clause reads ‘he was at the court like a chamberlain.’
segments.s747-g5: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. پژمرده شد = ‘became withered/faded/pale’ (پژمرده is an adjective ‘withered/pale’, شد = ‘became’). چادر آفتاب is a poetic genitive ‘the sun’s tent/pavilion’ (چادر = tent/covering, آفتاب = sun), so the clause means ‘when the sun’s tent faded’ (i.e., at dusk).
segments.s747-g6: همی‌ساخت is the imperfective/past‑habitual ‘was making’ or ‘would make’ (همی‑ + ساخت). هر = ‘every/each’. مهتری here means ‘an elder/chief’ (a person of rank). جای خواب is a compound ‘place of sleep’ (جای = place; خواب = sleep), i.e. a sleeping place or bed. Together: ‘each elder would prepare a place to sleep.’
segments.s747-g7: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past ‘he commanded/ordered’ (often used of a king). تا here functions as a subordinating conjunction ‘that/so that’ introducing the content of the order. پاسبانان شهر = ‘the guards/watchmen of the city’ (پاسبانان = guards, شهر = city).
segments.s747-g8: هر آنکس که = ‘whichever person who / whoever’. از مهتری داشت is an idiomatic expression: از + مهتری = ‘any claim to precedence/rank’, داشت = past ‘had’ (داشت here marks possession/claim). بهر = ‘for/for the purpose of’ (the sentence is left open and continues after this segment). The clause therefore sets up ‘whoever had any precedence…’ as the object/target of the order.
segments.s747-g9: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/departed' (subject implied). یکسر functions adverbially here as 'at once' (emphasizing the entirety/simultaneity of the action). سوی + noun is the standard prepositional phrase 'toward the ...' so the whole segment = 'they went at once toward the palace.'
segments.s747-g10: ب is the short preposition 'to/into' introducing the following locative; دان in the printed segmentation corresponds to the demonstrative element ('that') fused in older/poetic Persian (here treated as part of 'to that'), so its ordinary dictionary senses ('to know', 'to consider') do not apply (see senses map). جای 'place' is the head noun, followed by the coordinating nouns شادی و آرام 'joy and repose' and then the possessor شاه 'the king' (Persian places the possessor after the possessed), giving 'to that place of the king's joy and repose.'
segments.s747-g11: بدیشان is the prepositional phrase 'to them' (ب + ایشان). چنین گفت is a formulaic sequence 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); کامشب is a temporal adverb 'this night / tonight'. خروش 'uproar, tumult' is the direct object/topic of the reported speech and continues into the next segment.
segments.s747-g12: This clause shows poetic/inverted word order: دگرگونه‌تر (comparative/adverb 'more/differently') modifies کرد ('make/do'), while باید ('must/ought') gives the modal necessity; overall meaning is 'there must be/must make a different uproar.' ز دوش is a comparative/ablative phrase 'from last night' (ز = 'from', دوش = 'last night'), so the line says the uproar should be different from the one of last night.
segments.s747-g13: همه پاسبانان is the noun phrase 'all the guards/sentries' (همه = 'all/everyone' preposing the noun). بنام here means 'in the name of / invoking the name of' and is followed by the proper name قباد (Qobad). Grammatically this marks the manner or invocation under which the guards act.
segments.s747-g14: همی‌کرد is an imperfective/habitual past-form meaning 'would do / used to do' (the prefixed همی- marks continuous/habitual aspect). باید ('must / it is necessary') expresses obligation and here follows the verbal phrase in poetic inversion; بهر پاس یاد is a purpose/benefactive phrase 'for the sake of / as a remembrance of the sentry' (بهر = 'for', پاس = 'sentry/guard', یاد = 'remembrance').
segments.s747-g15: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed formula 'he gave such an answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces the reported/quoted clause ('that'); ای دون is a vocative address 'O base/ignoble one' (ای = vocative particle, دون = 'low/ignoble'), and کنم is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive 'shall I do / I should do.'
segments.s747-g16: ز سر is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from the head' (ز = 'from', سر = 'head'); نام پرویز = 'the name Parviz' (نام = 'name', پرویز = proper name). بیرون کنَم = 'I will put out / remove' (بیرون = 'out/away', کنم = 'I do/make' in subjunctive), so the clause literally asks 'Shall I remove the name Parviz from your head?'
segments.s747-g65: This phrase sets the location and the reason: به باغ بزرگ اندر = 'in/into the great garden'. اندر is a literary locative ('in/within'). از بس درخت is a compact causal phrase: از + بس + درخت = 'from/through so many trees' (literary 'because of the abundance of trees'). Word order is typical older Persian: locative (به باغ بزرگ اندر) followed by a causal prepositional phrase (از بس درخت).
segments.s747-g66: نبد is an archaic negative copula meaning 'there was not / did not exist'. شاه را uses the object/topic marker را to indicate the beneficiary/possessor ('for the king'). در چمن جای تخت groups the location and the noun: در چمن 'on/in the lawn' + جای تخت 'a place for the throne' (جای + تخت = 'place for the throne'). Together: 'there was no place on the lawn for the king's throne.'
segments.s747-g67: بیاویخت is a classical past verb meaning 'it/he/she hung' (bia-vīkht, a prefixed past). از marks origin/attachment here ('from/off'). شاخ زرین سپر = 'the golden bough/branch of the shield' (شاخ used poetically for a projecting piece). Word order: verb + origin phrase + qualifier of the object.
segments.s747-g68: بجایی here functions as a locative/adverbial 'in/to a place' (classical use of بجا), followed by a relative phrase کزو 'from which' + دور بودی گذر 'the road/passage was far'. بودی is past-tense 'was'. The whole segment gives the location: 'in a place from which the passage was far.'
segments.s747-g69: نشست is the simple past 'he sat' (classical narrative past). از before a list of flowers is literary and here roughly means 'among' or 'on/among the narcissus and saffron' — i.e. he took his seat amid those flowers. The conjunction و links the two floral nouns.
segments.s747-g70: This short clause describes an accompanying object: یکی تیغ = 'one (a) sword'. در زیر زانو is the locative phrase 'under the knee' (در + زیر + زانو); classical Persian often stacks locatives. گران is an adjectival predicate 'heavy/weighty', modifying the sword. The whole: 'a sword, heavy, in/under his knee.'
segments.s747-g71: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'. خورشید before the verb here is a simile or source of light ('like the sun' or 'as the sun'). برزد is an archaic past verb meaning here roughly 'struck/struck (with light)' or 'flashed' — i.e. the sun's light struck the spear. سنان is the noun 'spear'. از فراز means 'from on high' (از + فراز).
segments.s747-g72: سوی کاخ شد = 'turned/took its direction toward the palace' (سوی = toward, شد used as intransitive 'moved/turned'). دشمن is the noun 'foe/enemy'. دیو ساز is a compound epithet: دیو 'demon' + ساز 'device/means' (here used adjectivally/epithetically), giving a figurative compound like 'demon-making device/demon-like' — i.e. a fearsome, devilish foe. The clause says the foe moved toward the palace.
segments.s747-g73: یکایک (one by one) is an adverbial phrase placed at the start to quantify the following verb. بگشتند is the past-tense 3rd-person-plural verb 'they returned/turned'; گرد is the preposition 'around/about' governing سرای. The word order is typical Classical Persian: adverbial + verb + prepositional phrase 'around the palace'.
segments.s747-g74: تهی is an adjective 'empty'. بد here is an archaic poetic form: a shortened/contracted past copula (classical 'بود') meaning 'was' — so 'تهی بد' = 'was empty'. ز is the preposition 'from/of/because of' linking cause or source; شاه سرافراز جای is a genitive grouping 'the place/seat of the lofty (honored) king'.
segments.s747-g75: به تاراج دادند is a fixed/poetic verbal phrase meaning 'they gave to pillage / they plundered'. به is the preposition introducing the act of pillage, تاراج is the noun 'pillage', and دادند is the past 3rd-person-plural 'they gave' (here part of the idiom). گنج ورا is the object 'the treasure (of him/it)' with ورا ('to him/it' or 'of it') marking the possessor/target.
segments.s747-g76: نکرد is the negative past 'did not (do)'; here it negates the act of remembering. ایچ (poetic) = 'nothing / at all', and کس with this context means 'anyone' but combined (ایچ کس) gives 'no one at all'. یاد رنج ورا is the object phrase 'remembrance of his suffering' (یاد = memory/remembering, رنج = suffering, ورا = 'him/it').
segments.s747-g77: همه باز گشتند is a straightforward clause: 'everyone returned again' (همه = all, باز = again, گشتند = they returned). دیده پرآب is a postposed descriptive phrase 'eye(s) full of water' (literary for 'teary eyes'), where the noun (دیده) precedes the compound adjective (پرآب) as is common in Persian.
segments.s747-g78: گرفته is a past participle meaning 'taken/seized' or 'overcome' and functions here as a predicate adjective describing their state. ز is the preposition 'by/from'; کار زمانه is a genitive phrase 'the work/affairs of the age' (کار = work/affair, زمانه = the age/the times). شتاب (haste) at the end names the quality or cause: 'seized by the haste of the times.'
segments.s747-g79: چه جوییم is an interrogative-present/optative 'what shall we seek / what do we seek' (چه = what; جوییم = we seek / let us seek). ازین (از + این) = 'from this'; the provided token 'ین' is an orthographic variant of 'این' meaning 'this'. گنبد تیزگرد is a noun phrase 'dome (vault of heaven) that is swift/spinning' — the adjective تیزگرد follows the noun in classical word order.
segments.s747-g80: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. هرگز نیاساید is a negative jussive/subjunctive sense 'never may rest / never rests' (هرگز = never; نیاساید = may not/does not repose). از کارکرد 'from its work/operation' gives the cause or sphere: کارکرد here = 'workings/performance' of the dome (i.e., the ongoing motions of the heavens).
segments.s747-g89: 'اگر' introduces a counterfactual conditional ('if'). 'خود' is an emphatic reflexive ('yourself'). 'نزادی' is the past form of 'زادیدن' ('to beget/give birth') used here in the protasis ('had sired'). 'خردمند' is an adjective placed before the noun 'مرد' to form 'a wise man'.
segments.s747-g90: 'نبودی' is the past (apodosis) form of 'بودن' used in the unreal/counterfactual sense ('would not have been'/'was not'). 'ورا' is an enclitic/oblique pronoun meaning 'for him/it'. The noun phrase 'روز ننگ و نبرد' ('a day of shame and battle') follows the pronoun; Persian often places the verb before these objects in classical poetic word order.
segments.s747-g91: 'ندیدی' is the negative past/perfect of 'دیدن' ('you/one has not seen'), used here as a general statement. 'جهان' is the direct object 'the world'. The prepositional phrase 'از بنه به بدی' ('from the root/starting-point into evil') qualifies the extent of corruption; note that in classical usage 'بنه' is used poetically as 'origin' (sense not listed in the provided options).
segments.s747-g92: This is a pair of conditional clauses introduced by 'اگر' ('if'). 'اگر که' is a reinforced conditional ('if it be that'). 'بدی مرد' is a noun phrase 'an evil man' (adjective before noun). The second 'اگر مه بدی' repeats the conditional structure: 'if the moon is evil' — 'مه' is the poetic word for 'moon'. Both clauses offer possible causes (man or moon) for the world's evil; the verbs are omitted (understood 'is'/'exists'), common in poetic Persian.
segments.s747-g93: کنون is an adverbial opening meaning 'now'. رنج is the noun 'hardship/toil' as the direct object of the action. در here is the simple preposition 'in' and کار is 'affair/matter', so در کار 'in the affair/affairs'. خسرو is the proper name 'Khosrow' modifying کار. بریم is a first-person-plural verbal form (classical Persian subjunctive/optative or hortative sense) from بردن meaning 'let us take/let us bear' or 'we will take on', so the clause reads literally 'Now (let) we take/undertake the hardship in Khosrow's affair.' Word order is adverb + object phrase + verb (typical for literary Persian where the verb ends the clause).
segments.s747-g94: بخواننده is the dative form 'to the reader' (the prefix بـ marks the indirect object). آگاهی is the direct object 'information/account/notice' that will be given. نوبریم is a first-person-plural verbal construction formed from نو- (new/novel) + a b-verb, here functioning like 'we present/bring as new' or 'we offer as a novelty'; grammatically it is the verb governing آگاهی and takes بخواننده as the indirect object. The clause order is indirect object + direct object + verb, a normal pattern in Persian.
segments.s747-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. خسرو is the subject (proper name). بدان گونه is a fused demonstrative phrase ('to/into that manner' = 'that kind of'), with ب marking direction/connection and دان here representing the archaic element of 'آن' (the demonstrative); گونه is 'manner/kind'. آوا is the direct object 'sound/voice'. شنید is the simple past verb 'he heard' (3rd-person past). The whole segment = 'when Khosrow heard that kind of sound.'
segments.s747-g34: به رخساره شد is a stative result: به marks the domain ('on/to his face'), رخساره = 'face/complexion', شد = past 'became'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', گل = 'flower', شنبلید names a particular delicate plant/flower (shenbalid) used here in a simile. Together: 'his face became like a shenbalid flower.'
segments.s747-g35: چنین گفت is a fixed narrative formula 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت here is the verb 'said' in past). کز = archaic fused form (که + ز) introducing reported content, often 'that' or 'from which'; شب گذشته = 'the night that passed/last night'; سه پاس = 'three (pās) watches' (پاس = a watch/guard/watch-division of the night). Word order is typical classical Persian: formulaic preface + reported clause.
segments.s747-g36: بیابید here is an archaic/poetic verbal form used in reported speech; it functions to introduce what is found/reported (cf. impersonal 'it is found/it appears' or an older subjunctive/imperative form used narratively). گفتار = 'utterance/saying', and اخترشناس = 'the astrologer' (the source of the report). The phrase links the reported number of watches to the astrologer's statement.
segments.s747-g37: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that'. این بد گهر is a nominal phrase 'this ill-fated/ill-born jewel' (گهر used metaphorically for a child). تا ز مادر بزاد = 'was born from the mother' with تا used here in the temporal/scope sense ('that/when/by the time'), ز = poetic 'from', and بزاد is the past form 'was born'. The clause states the birth event of the referred child.
segments.s747-g38: نهانی is an adverb 'secretly/hiddenly' modifying the speaker's action. و is the conjunction 'and'. را is the definite/object marker (here marking the object of named action). نام کردم is a compound where نام + کردن = 'to name' and کردم is 1st-person past 'I made/did' = 'I named'. قباد is the given name (object of the action). Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic but reads: 'and secretly I named him Qobad.'
segments.s747-g39: به آواز شیرویه is an adverbial phrase 'in the voice/of the voice of Shiruyeh' (به used adverbially here). گفتم is 1st-person past 'I said'. همی is an archaic particle often adding emphasis or continuous aspect to the verb in classical Persian; here it intensifies or marks the utterance. Together: 'I said in Shiruyeh's voice.'
segments.s747-g40: دگر modifies نامش: 'another/other name of his' (دگر = 'another', نامش = 'his name' with the enclitic ش). اندر is the preposition 'in/within'. نهفتم is the 1st-person past 'I hid' (archaic form), so اندر نهفتم = 'I hid (it) in/within'. همی again is the archaic particle indicating the habitual/continuous or emphatic sense. The whole clause: 'I gave him another name and hid it.' (poetic inversion places modifiers before the verb.)
segments.s747-g17: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' شب is the noun 'night' as the time‑setting. چادر قیرگون ('veil pitch-black') is a noun phrase where چادر (veil/cloak) is qualified by the adjective قیرگون. کرد is the past tense verb 'made/caused' (here: 'drew/made into'), and نو (literally 'new') functions adverbially after the verb meaning 'anew' or 'freshly.' The phrase as a whole is a temporal subordinate clause: 'When night made its pitch-black veil anew...'.
segments.s747-g18: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' 'ز شهر و ز بازار' is a paired prepositional phrase 'from the city and from the bazaar.' برخاست is the past tense verb 'rose/arose,' and غو is the noun 'tumult/uproar' that is the thing which arose; word order is somewhat inverse (verb before its noun) as common in poetry: 'from the city and market arose a tumult.'
segments.s747-g19: همه پاسبانان is the subject 'all the guards.' بنام قباد is an adverbial/parenthetical phrase 'in the name of Qobad'—idiomatically here 'calling out Qobad's name.' بنام functions like a prepositional idiom rather than a passive label ('named').
segments.s747-g20: چو is 'when' introducing a temporal clause. آواز دادند is an idiom 'they gave a cry/they shouted' where دادند (here) means 'emit/utter' (to shout). کردند یاد is the compound verbal expression 'یاد کردن' with کردند as the auxiliary 'they did/made' so the object 'یاد' (remembrance) becomes verbalized: 'they made (him) remember' or 'they caused remembrance.' The object of 'remind' is left implicit in the line.
segments.s747-g21: شب تیره is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'the dark night.' شاه جهان is the title 'king of the world' (noun + epithet) serving as subject. خفته بود is the predicate: خفته (asleep) plus the past auxiliary بود 'was' — a past-state construction meaning 'was asleep.'
segments.s747-g22: چو again introduces a temporal clause 'when.' شیرین is a proper name (Shirin). به بالینش is a locative phrase 'at his bedside' where بالین + possessive suffix -ش means 'his pillow/side.' بر جفته بود is a past verbal phrase: بر here is a prepositional/locative particle and جفته (poetic/archaic) with بود forms a perfective state 'had lain/was lying together/close by' — idiomatically 'she was at his bedside.'
segments.s747-g23: چو is 'when' (temporal). آواز آن پاسبانان is a genitive construction 'the cry/voice of those guards' (آواز + آن پاسبانان). شنید is the simple past 'he heard' (3rd person singular); the subject ('he' — the king) is implied from the prior clause.
segments.s747-g24: غمی گشت is a small clause 'a sorrow became' = 'sorrow came upon him' (غمی indefinite subject + گشت 'became'). و links the next result clause. زیشان ('from them' / 'because of them') specifies source or cause. دلش بردمید is an idiomatic passive/experiential phrase: دلش ('his heart') + بردمید (an archaic verbal form meaning 'was taken/seized') — 'his heart was seized/taken away (by them).' Together: 'sorrow fell on him and his heart was seized by them.'
segments.s747-g57: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. روشن شود is a verbal phrase (روشن 'clear' + شود 3sg of شدن) meaning 'becomes clear/known' (classical present/future sense). دشمن چاره‌جوی is the clause's subject and descriptor: دشمن 'the enemy' and چاره‌جوی ('one who seeks a remedy' = 'seeking a way') functioning as an adjectival/participial noun; word order is classical (temporal clause + subject phrase).
segments.s747-g58: نهد is the 3sg present/future of نهاد here used as a verb 'sets/places' in the idiom نهادنِ روی = 'to set/turn (one's) face'. بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'. سوی این کاخ روی is the goal phrase 'toward this palace' with روی 'face' used idiomatically ('turn his face toward'); classical word order places the verb before its complements.
segments.s747-g59: هم آنگه is a temporal adverbial 'then/at once'. زره 'armor' is the direct object. خواست is the past 3sg of خواستن used here as 'requested/asked for'. از گنج شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the king’s treasure/treasury' specifying the source.
segments.s747-g60: This is an enumerated list of the items requested. دو شمشیر = 'two swords'; the adjectives follow the noun in classical Persian, so شمشیر هندی و رومی reads 'swords, Indian and Roman' (here best understood as 'one Indian and one Roman sword'), and کلاه is an additional item 'a helmet' (کلاه here = helmet). Punctuation is omitted in the original, so the line strings the items together.
segments.s747-g61: همان introduces 'the same/that very' item. ترکش (printed here as ترک + ش) is the classical word for 'quiver' (container for arrows); the tokenization splits it but the sense in context is 'quiver of arrows'. تیرو = 'of arrows' (تیرو = arrow with ezāfe sense), زرین سپر = 'golden shield' ('زرین' postmodifies سپر). The line lists further equipment following the previous swords and helmet.
segments.s747-g62: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain'. بنده is 'servant/slave' and is the head noun; گرد and پرخاشخر are predicative adjectives describing that servant. پرخاشخر means 'quarrelsome/aggressive'; گرد in this passage functions as a descriptive adjective (the manuscript’s brief lexical list does not give the exact sense here), so the phrase means 'a certain servant, (round/robust) and quarrelsome.'
segments.s747-g63: شب تیره‌گون = 'a dark/gloomy night' (adjective تیره‌گون follows the noun). اندر آمد به باغ is the verb phrase 'came into the garden' with اندر 'into/within' and آمد 'came' and به باغ marking the location; classical poetic word order places the temporal/qualifying phrase before the verb.
segments.s747-g64: بدان گه که is a fixed temporal phrase 'at that time when' (بدان + گه + relative که). برخیزد is 3sg present/subjunctive of برخاستن 'to rise/get up' used here narratively. ازخواب = 'from sleep' and زاغ = 'rook/crow'; together the clause means 'at that moment when the rook awoke from sleep.'

sentences.s747-s21: Thereupon his name 'Bad-Shiruy' became manifest; Qobad, this attendant, used to call him so.
sentences.s747-s22: A dark night must fall toward China — or, if toward us, toward China and the land of Makran.
sentences.s747-s23: By sorcery we will seize their road; I will request an army from the Chinese faghfur.
sentences.s747-s24: Because of that, his pigeon was dark against the sky; his words lay astonished upon the earth.
sentences.s747-s41: To one he gives the royal crown; to another he gives (him) to the sea as a fish.
sentences.s747-s42: To one (he gives) naked of head and feet and tightly bound; neither rest nor food nor a place to hide (has he).
sentences.s747-s43: To one he gives a draught, honey and milk; he clothes (another) in brocade, velvet and silk.
sentences.s747-s44: In the end both are in the earth; they lie in the dark snare of destruction.
sentences.s747-s13: He said to him, 'O king, what can be done about the body? Upon this matter one must take action.'
sentences.s747-s14: At the sound of that voice the king awoke; because of those words his heart became full of distress.
sentences.s747-s15: To Shirin he said thus: 'O moon-faced one, why are you sleeping inside?'
sentences.s747-s16: He said to Shirin, 'Open your ears — listen to the guards' cry.'
sentences.s747-s25: The dark, enchanted night proved of no use; instead a hard and humiliating task kept coming upon him.
sentences.s747-s26: To Shirin he said thus: 'The time has come — suspicion has prevailed over our enchantment.'
sentences.s747-s27: To him Shirin said: 'May a draught of evil be always far from you; may the hand of evil remain distant from you.'
sentences.s747-s28: Know this now: prepare your remedy; lest the enemy come in need.
sentences.s747-s1: That same fortunate-born one stood in the court, and he gave no one passage.
sentences.s747-s2: When he (or one) became aware of that word of the king, he was at the court like a chamberlain.
sentences.s747-s3: When the sun’s pavilion grew pale, every elder made a place for sleep.
sentences.s747-s4: He commanded that the city’s guards — whoever among them had any precedence — (should ...).
sentences.s747-s5: They all went at once toward the palace, to that place of the king's joy and repose.
sentences.s747-s6: He said to them thus: 'Tonight the uproar must be of a different sort than last night.'
sentences.s747-s7: All the guards, invoking Qobad's name, did so; it ought to be, as a memorial for the sentry.
sentences.s747-s8: He answered thus: 'O base one, shall I take the name "Parviz" from off your head?'
sentences.s747-s33: In the great garden, because of so many trees there was no place on the lawn for the king's throne.
sentences.s747-s34: It hung from the golden bough of the shield, in a spot from which the path was distant.
sentences.s747-s35: He sat amid narcissus and saffron; a sword lay heavy beneath his knee.
sentences.s747-s36: When, from on high, the spear flashed like the sun, the foe — a devil-made device — turned toward the palace.
sentences.s747-s37: One by one they turned about the palace; the place of the lofty king lay empty.
sentences.s747-s38: They plundered the treasure, and no one at all remembered his suffering.
sentences.s747-s39: All returned again, eyes full of water (teary), seized by the haste of the times.
sentences.s747-s40: What shall we seek from this swiftly turning dome, which never rests from its workings?
sentences.s747-s45: If you had not yourself sired a wise man, there would not have been for him a day of shame and battle.
sentences.s747-s46: You have not seen the world so corrupt from its root; whether an evil man (is to blame) or whether the moon is evil.
sentences.s747-s47: Now let us take up the hardship in Khosrow's affairs and present to the reader a new account.
sentences.s747-s17: When Khosrow heard that kind of sound, his face became like a shenbalid flower.
sentences.s747-s18: He said thus that, the astrologer reported, three watches of the past night had passed.
sentences.s747-s19: that this ill-born jewel was born from the mother, and secretly I named him Qobad.
sentences.s747-s20: In Shiruyeh's voice I said, 'I gave him another name and kept it hidden.'
sentences.s747-s9: When night drew its pitch-black veil anew, a tumult rose from the city and the bazaar.
sentences.s747-s10: All the guards, calling out Qobad's name, when they gave a cry, reminded (him).
sentences.s747-s11: On the dark night the king of the world was asleep, when Shirin had lain at his bedside.
sentences.s747-s12: When he heard the cry of those guards, sorrow came upon him and his heart was seized by them.
sentences.s747-s29: When it becomes known, the enemy — searching for a way — will surely turn his face toward this palace.
sentences.s747-s30: Immediately he asked the king’s treasury for armor: two swords — one Indian and one Roman — and a helmet.
sentences.s747-s31: Also (he took) a quiver of arrows and a golden shield, and a certain servant — stout and quarrelsome.
sentences.s747-s32: A dark night came into the garden at that moment when the rook rose from sleep.
